claims.claim_text: Kubernetes was heavily inspired by Google's internal Borg system.
claims.span_hint: section: The Scaling Challenge, Did You Know block
claims.rationale: The upstream Kubernetes blog explicitly describes Borg as Kubernetes' predecessor and source of core ideas.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes is widely used for production workloads, including machine learning systems.
claims.span_hint: section: The Scaling Challenge, Did You Know block
claims.rationale: The case-study hub supports broad real-world production use, but it is not a precise measure of overall adoption or ML-specific prevalence.

claims.claim_text: Pods are disposable, and higher-level workload controllers are what recreate replacements after node or pod failure.
claims.rationale: The workloads overview explains that Pods are disposable and that workload resources manage the desired set of Pods.

claims.claim_text: A Kubernetes Service provides a stable network endpoint for a changing set of backend Pods.
claims.rationale: The Service docs directly describe Services as exposing an application running as one or more Pods behind a single endpoint.

claims.claim_text: The NVIDIA GPU Operator automates cluster-wide setup of GPU software components such as drivers, the container toolkit, device plugin, and monitoring components.
claims.span_hint: section: GPU Scheduling for ML, NVIDIA GPU Operator
claims.rationale: This is a real product-capability claim, but the primary source is NVIDIA documentation on an off-allowlist host.

claims.claim_text: NVIDIA Multi-Instance GPU can partition an A100 into up to seven isolated GPU instances.
claims.span_hint: section: GPU Resource Types, Did You Know block
claims.rationale: The exact partitioning limit is documented by NVIDIA, but that documentation is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: CPU limits are enforced by throttling, while memory limits can trigger OOM kills under memory pressure.
claims.span_hint: section: Resource Management, Resource Requests vs Limits
claims.rationale: The resource-management docs explicitly distinguish CPU throttling from reactive memory OOM enforcement.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes QoS classes affect eviction order under node pressure, with BestEffort evicted before Burstable and Guaranteed.
claims.rationale: The Pod QoS docs describe the three classes and their role in eviction decisions.

claims.claim_text: The Horizontal Pod Autoscaler periodically adjusts a workload's desired replica count based on observed resource or custom metrics.
claims.span_hint: section: Autoscaling for ML, Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA)
claims.rationale: The HPA documentation states that the controller periodically adjusts desired scale based on metrics.

claims.claim_text: The Vertical Pod Autoscaler adjusts Pod resource requests and limits based on observed usage, rather than adding replicas.
claims.span_hint: section: Autoscaling for ML, Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA)
claims.rationale: The official VPA docs directly explain vertical scaling and how VPA updates requests and limits.

claims.claim_text: ReadWriteOncePod is the access mode used to guarantee that only one Pod in the cluster can write to a volume.
claims.span_hint: section: Persistent Storage for ML, Access Modes, Did You Know block
claims.rationale: The ReadWriteOncePod task page explicitly explains that this mode is for strict single-writer access by one Pod.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes provides built-in DNS-based service discovery, including names like service.namespace.svc.cluster.local.
claims.span_hint: section: Networking Deep Dive for ML Services, DNS Resolution
claims.rationale: The DNS documentation explains how Services and Pods receive cluster DNS names and how namespace-qualified lookups work.

claims.claim_text: Topology Aware Routing prefers same-zone traffic and can improve reliability, performance, or cost.
claims.span_hint: section: Networking Deep Dive for ML Services, Latency Optimization Strategies
claims.rationale: The feature page states that topology-aware routing helps keep traffic within a zone and may improve latency, throughput, reliability, or cost.

claims.claim_text: A readiness probe keeps a Pod out of Service traffic until it is ready, and a failed readiness probe removes the Pod from matching service endpoints.
claims.rationale: The probes documentation directly describes readiness behavior and endpoint removal.

claims.claim_text: Pods can remain Pending when the scheduler cannot place them due to insufficient resources, and Cluster Autoscaler only scales when a suitable added node would make them schedulable.
claims.rationale: The Cluster Autoscaler FAQ explains that scale-up is triggered by unschedulable Pods and only when adding a similar node would help.

claims.claim_text: Guaranteed QoS is achieved when every container has CPU and memory requests equal to limits, making those Pods least likely to be evicted.
claims.rationale: The Pod QoS page states both the criteria for Guaranteed QoS and its eviction priority advantages.

claims.claim_text: Taints repel Pods from nodes unless they have matching tolerations, which is how teams keep general workloads off specialized GPU nodes.
claims.rationale: The taints and tolerations docs explicitly describe taints as repelling non-tolerating Pods from nodes.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Components
further_reading.why_relevant: Best upstream overview of control plane and node components referenced throughout the module.

further_reading.title: Schedule GPUs
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers the official Kubernetes model for requesting GPUs and scheduling workloads onto accelerator nodes.

further_reading.title: Persistent Volumes
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains access modes such as RWX and RWOP that appear in the storage and knowledge-check sections.

further_reading.title: Horizontal Pod Autoscaling
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful follow-on reading for the autoscaling examples, custom metrics discussion, and HPA behavior settings.

notes: Excluded Mermaid diagrams, tables, quiz prompts, and code blocks from claim extraction per instructions. Two content-review issues surfaced outside the citation scope: the same-namespace DNS example uses 127.0.0.1 instead of a Service DNS name, and the Service example using topologyKeys reflects an older API pattern rather than current topology-aware routing guidance.
